blurb: DNS, TCP/UDP, HTTP/2 vs 3, CDN, WebSockets.

questions.q: HTTP/2 on a single TCP connection. What causes head-of-line blocking?
choices: TCP-level packet loss blocks ALL multiplexed streams until retransmit
questions.explanation: HTTP/2 multiplexes N streams over 1 TCP connection. But TCP is a single ordered byte stream — any packet loss pauses all streams until retransmit. HTTP/3 (over QUIC/UDP) solves this with per-stream loss recovery.

questions.q: A client needs real-time bidirectional push from server. Which is best?
questions.explanation: WebSocket: full-duplex, persistent. SSE: server→client only. Polling/long-poll: many HTTP requests. For bidirectional chat, WebSocket is the canonical choice.

questions.q: DNS TTL of 300s for a primary; primary dies. How long until resolver clients fully recover to failover IP?
questions.explanation: Until TTL expires, resolvers serve the dead IP. Plus resolver-cache propagation. Low-TTL (30–60s) faster failover; tradeoff: more DNS query load.

questions.q: Anycast routing: all CDN PoPs announce the same IP. Which ROUTING decision sends the user's packet to the nearest PoP?
choices: BGP (internet routers prefer topologically closest announcement)
questions.explanation: Each PoP BGP-announces the same prefix. Internet routers naturally prefer the shortest AS path. PoP failure = withdraw BGP = traffic re-routes automatically. Cloudflare's core trick.

questions.q: TCP connection handshake: how many RTTs before data can flow?
choices: 1.5 (SYN + SYN-ACK + ACK; data can piggyback on ACK)
questions.explanation: SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK = 1.5 RTT. Data can ride the final ACK (TCP Fast Open). QUIC (HTTP/3) combines handshake + first data in 0-RTT for resumed sessions.

questions.q: A reverse proxy (nginx/envoy) vs a forward proxy. Which one sits in FRONT of the app servers?
questions.explanation: Reverse proxy (nginx, ALB, Cloudflare): accepts client requests, fans out to backend pool. Forward proxy (Squid, corporate proxy): accepts internal-client requests, forwards to external internet.

questions.q: HTTP/3 is built on which transport?
questions.explanation: HTTP/3 = HTTP semantics over QUIC, and QUIC is built on UDP. Per-stream loss recovery, 0-RTT resumption, connection migration across network changes.

questions.q: Your TLS certificate expires at 3 AM on a Saturday. The on-call engineer doesn't notice. By Monday morning, all browsers show 'Your connection is not private' and API clients are refusing to connect. What's the immediate fix AND the long-term prevention?
choices: Immediately renew/replace the cert; long-term: use automated cert management (Let's Encrypt + certbot auto-renew) with monitoring alerts at 30/14/7 days before expiry
questions.explanation: Expired TLS certs cause hard failures — browsers and HTTPS clients refuse the connection entirely. Automated certificate management (ACME/Let's Encrypt) with renewal at 30 days before expiry eliminates manual rotation. Alert on cert-expiry-days-remaining metric as a safety net.

questions.q: You change your primary database IP and update DNS, but your DNS records had a 24-hour TTL. For the next day, some users hit the old IP and get connection refused. What should you have done?
choices: Lower the TTL to 60 seconds days BEFORE the migration, wait for the old TTL to expire, then perform the IP change, then optionally raise TTL back
Notify all users to flush DNS
questions.explanation: The standard DNS migration playbook: step 1, lower TTL well in advance (at least old-TTL duration before cutover). Step 2, wait for all caches to expire the old high-TTL record. Step 3, change the IP. Step 4, verify. Step 5, optionally raise TTL again. Skipping step 1 means resolvers cache the old IP for the full original TTL.

questions.q: Your gRPC service uses HTTP/2 with a single long-lived connection to a backend behind an L4 load balancer. You scale the backend from 2 to 10 pods. Requests still only hit the original 2 pods. Why?
choices: L4 (TCP) load balancers route per-connection, not per-request; the single persistent HTTP/2 connection was established to one backend and all multiplexed streams go there
questions.explanation: L4 load balancers operate at the TCP level. Once a connection is established, all traffic on it goes to the same backend. HTTP/2 multiplexes many requests over one connection, so new backends never see traffic. Fix: use an L7 (HTTP-aware) load balancer like Envoy, or implement client-side load balancing with gRPC name resolution.

questions.q: During a regional failover, your CDN continues serving stale content from edge caches even though the origin is now in a different region with updated data. Users see data from 6 hours ago. What went wrong?
choices: Cache-Control max-age was set too high and no cache purge/invalidation was triggered as part of the failover runbook
questions.explanation: CDN edge caches serve content until max-age expires or a purge is issued. If your failover runbook doesn't include a CDN purge/invalidation step, edges keep serving stale content from the old origin. Always include CDN invalidation in failover procedures, and consider stale-while-revalidate for graceful transitions.

questions.q: A mobile app works fine on WiFi but fails intermittently on cellular with 'connection reset' errors. TCP connections drop when the user moves between cell towers. Which protocol feature solves this?
choices: QUIC connection migration: connections survive IP address changes because they're identified by connection ID, not IP:port tuple
questions.explanation: TCP connections are bound to a 4-tuple (src IP:port, dst IP:port). When a mobile device switches cell towers, its IP changes, killing all TCP connections. QUIC (HTTP/3) uses a connection ID independent of IP, so the connection survives network changes transparently. This is a major reason Google pushed for QUIC on mobile.
